does: This paper proposes SCRUB-FL, a two-phase defense mechanism against backdoor attacks in federated learning that operates both during and after training. During the training phase, clients identify suspicious samples through spectral analysis and activation clustering, enabling them to detect potential backdoor triggers without knowledge of the attack pattern. The method then applies unlearning techniques to remove backdoor behaviors from the converged global model in a post-training sanitization phase. The approach addresses the fundamental challenge that existing defenses operating only at aggregation cannot fully eliminate backdoors that persist in the final model, particularly when the server lacks knowledge of trigger patterns or poisoned client identities.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research on backdoor attacks and defenses in federated learning, extending prior work that focused on aggregation-time detection methods. It responds to limitations in earlier post-training sanitization approaches that suffer from neuron entanglement and rely on explicit knowledge of triggers. The paper combines multiple defense mechanisms: spectral analysis and activation clustering draw from anomaly detection literature in neural networks, while the unlearning component connects to recent work on machine unlearning for removing unwanted model behaviors. It addresses a gap where previous defenses either detect attacks early or attempt remediation late, but struggle with the persistent backdoor problem after model convergence in the decentralized FL setting.
enables: This work enables more robust post-training defense pipelines for federated learning systems and opens directions for practical backdoor mitigation without requiring explicit trigger knowledge or identification of compromised clients. The spectral and clustering-based anomaly detection approach could extend to other types of poisoning attacks beyond backdoors. Future work could explore how unlearning techniques scale across heterogeneous client models and data distributions in FL, and whether the method can be adapted for continual learning scenarios where new backdoors may emerge after deployment. This establishes foundations for privacy-preserving collaborative learning systems that can tolerate adversarial participation and recover from attacks post-convergence, potentially applicable to sensitive domains like healthcare and finance where backdoor resilience is critical.
